caveats: a: Occupation sources span multiple industries and do not provide NAICS 924120 wage weights.
a: The code mixes land, forest, wildlife, geological, recreation, and other conservation programs with different task profiles.
a: The frozen labor-share input is missing because government activity has no compatible receipts denominator.
rho: No five-year implementation cohort specific to conservation-program administration was found.
rho: Technical feasibility in USGS programs does not establish adoption across smaller state and local agencies.
e: Government agencies may outsource substantial work, but the vendors are not establishments in this code.
e: The frozen dataset declares no defensible LMM firm count.
s5: Leadership succession is not ownership succession.
s5: Agency restructuring does not create an acquirable company.
q: Public budget savings and improved conservation outcomes are not equivalent to retained EBITDA benefit.
q: A neutral numeric assumption would conceal inapplicability.
d5: Employment projections are occupation-level and include private and government employers outside this industry.
d5: LWCF is only one conservation funding stream and funding authorization does not directly measure administrative output.
d5: Policy changes can shift program scope quickly even when environmental need persists.
o: This is a bounded institutional judgment rather than an observed operator-share measure.
o: The estimate concerns accountable operation, not headcount; a high value is compatible with significant labor efficiency.
o: A high public-operator requirement does not imply a private acquisition opportunity.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: These agencies serve the public, regulated parties, landowners, and other government bodies through appropriated budgets, statutory programs, grants, permits, regulation, monitoring, planning, and stewardship. They do not earn recurring commercial service revenue or normalized EBITDA from external customers.
lens.deviation_from_default: The default outsourced-service LMM lens cannot be populated because Census defines this code as government establishments and the frozen dataset has neither a receipts denominator nor a defensible LMM firm count. The full code is retained only to expose the structural mismatch, not narrowed to private conservation vendors in other NAICS industries.
lens.excluded_activities: Private environmental consultants, surveyors, mapping providers, forestry contractors, park operators, preserves, timber operators, laboratories, and technology vendors classified in other industries, as well as shells and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Government establishments administering, regulating, supervising, and controlling land use, natural-resource conservation, erosion control, geological survey programs, public and private forest protection, and fish, game, and wildlife conservation are retained for operational analysis; no commercial LMM firm population fits the code.

a.bridge: BLS describes conservation scientists and foresters, environmental science and protection technicians, and wildlife biologists across government and private employers rather than the complete wage-weighted occupation mix of NAICS 924120. I translate their mix of data analysis, GIS, modeling, reporting, planning, contract and permit work, public communication, field sampling, inspection, and professional judgment into moderate exposure. This likely overstates substitution for field inspection, sampling, enforcement, stakeholder negotiation, and statutory decisions, but may understate it for grants, permits, records, mapping, monitoring, and report-intensive agency units.
caveats: Occupation sources span multiple industries and do not provide NAICS 924120 wage weights.
The code mixes land, forest, wildlife, geological, recreation, and other conservation programs with different task profiles.
The frozen labor-share input is missing because government activity has no compatible receipts denominator.
a.change_evidence: A 924120-specific payroll-weighted task census spanning federal, state, and local conservation agencies, with hours by occupation, field versus desk work, current geospatial automation, and contractor use, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: AI can assist image interpretation, habitat and land-change detection, record search, plan and permit review, grant administration, reporting, correspondence, and analytical modeling. BLS documents extensive data, GIS, drone, satellite, reporting, and planning work alongside physical field tasks [S2][S3][S4]. USGS is already advancing Earth observation through AI and cloud access [S6].

d5.bridge: BLS occupation projections and statutory program funding measure conservation-related employment and one funding stream, not constant-price, constant-quality quantity for the complete 924120 administrative service basket. I translate 2024–34 projected growth of 3% for conservation scientists and foresters, 4% for environmental science technicians, and 2% for wildlife biologists, together with permanent annual LWCF funding, into modest five-year demand growth. This may overstate realized workload if government budgets, staffing, or policy priorities contract, or understate it if land-use pressure, wildfire, biodiversity loss, and monitoring mandates accelerate.
caveats: Employment projections are occupation-level and include private and government employers outside this industry.
LWCF is only one conservation funding stream and funding authorization does not directly measure administrative output.
Policy changes can shift program scope quickly even when environmental need persists.
d5.change_evidence: Deflated NAICS 924120 expenditures, program transactions and caseloads, acres monitored or regulated, permits and grants administered, field inspections, and enacted five-year federal and state budgets would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: BLS expects modest growth in several conservation-related occupations and identifies environmental pressure, habitat protection, and monitoring needs as drivers, while also warning of government budget constraints [S2][S3][S4]. The Land and Water Conservation Fund has $900 million in permanent annual authorization, providing one durable funding anchor [S8].

caveats: Government agencies may outsource substantial work, but the vendors are not establishments in this code.
The frozen dataset declares no defensible LMM firm count.
e.change_evidence: Only an explicit lens change to public entities or reassignment to NAICS codes covering private environmental, mapping, forestry, or program-support contractors would make eligibility measurable.
e.rationale: The industry is composed of government establishments, not firms with normalized EBITDA, and the frozen data provide no LMM firm denominator. Private conservation-service providers are classified elsewhere, so estimating an eligible share within 924120 would manufacture a nonexistent commercial population.

l.detail: No receipts concept for this code (government / SUSB out of scope). QCEW government wages exist ($18,364,842,924) but there is no revenue denominator; labor_share left null.
l.rationale: dataset labor_share has no defensible value

n.detail: margin 0.0000 (margins.json[92]: ESTIMATE: public administration — EBITDA is not meaningful for government units; margin 0 makes the n_band chain yield 0 by construction (recorded as a known gap)); revenue/employee $374,583 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at --); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: This is a bounded institutional judgment rather than an observed operator-share measure.
The estimate concerns accountable operation, not headcount; a high value is compatible with significant labor efficiency.
A high public-operator requirement does not imply a private acquisition opportunity.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of statutory delegation to automated systems, sustained agency elimination or consolidation, customer self-service replacing current approvals, or validated autonomous monitoring accepted without professional review would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Most year-five quantity still requires an accountable public operator to exercise regulatory authority, approve permits and grants, make stewardship choices, validate scientific evidence, conduct enforcement and field work, and answer to courts and the public. Remote sensing, automated monitoring, and self-service data can eliminate portions of manual observation and administration but cannot independently exercise sovereign authority or bear accountability.

caveats: Public budget savings and improved conservation outcomes are not equivalent to retained EBITDA benefit.
A neutral numeric assumption would conceal inapplicability.
q.change_evidence: Contract pricing, renewal, and benefit-sharing evidence would become relevant only for private vendors in different NAICS codes and under a redefined lens.
q.rationale: The frozen lens contains no commercial operator, customer price, contract repricing, or investor-retained gross benefit. Administrative savings may increase program capacity or reduce public cost, but they are not commercial value retention as this primitive defines it.

caveats: No five-year implementation cohort specific to conservation-program administration was found.
Technical feasibility in USGS programs does not establish adoption across smaller state and local agencies.
rho.change_evidence: Production adoption and audited hours-saved data from federal, state, and local conservation agencies, including false-positive rates, field-validation requirements, permit appeal outcomes, procurement time, and system integration cost, would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Existing GIS, Landsat, remote-sensing, cloud, and monitoring infrastructure makes substantial five-year implementation plausible [S5][S6]. Implementation is constrained by procurement, fragmented data, cybersecurity and privacy, scientific validation, public records, accessibility, environmental review, statutory procedures, field verification, model drift, and accountable professional judgment. GAO's federal evidence shows both fast AI use-case growth and persistent policy, resource, and governance obstacles [S7].

caveats: Leadership succession is not ownership succession.
Agency restructuring does not create an acquirable company.
s5.change_evidence: This primitive would require a methodology change or a different industry containing private contractors; no ordinary succession or deal dataset can make it applicable to government establishments.
s5.rationale: Conservation agencies cannot undergo qualifying private control transfers. Elections, appointments, reorganizations, interagency transfers, and program consolidations are not acquisitions of transferable LMM operating companies.

principal_driver: Data-intensive conservation monitoring and administration are well suited to geospatial and workflow automation while environmental needs persist.
principal_weakness: Government-only establishments provide no transferable LMM firm population or commercial benefit-retention mechanism.

sources.supports: Defines 924120 as government establishments administering and regulating land use, natural-resource conservation, erosion control, geological survey programs, forest lands, and fish and wildlife conservation.
sources.title: NAICS Sector 92 Definitions: 924120 Administration of Conservation Programs

sources.supports: Describes planning, compliance, contracts, monitoring, field and fire work, and use of drones, satellite imagery, and GIS; projects 3% combined employment growth from 2024 to 2034.
sources.title: Conservation Scientists and Foresters

sources.supports: Documents environmental monitoring, inspection, sample and data work across office, laboratory, and field settings and projects 4% employment growth from 2024 to 2034.
sources.title: Environmental Science and Protection Technicians

sources.supports: Describes GIS, modeling, fieldwork, conservation planning, and a workforce concentrated in government; projects 2% employment growth and identifies budget constraints.
sources.title: Zoologists and Wildlife Biologists

sources.supports: Documents annual 30-meter Landsat-based monitoring from 1985 onward used for ecosystem health, wildfire risk, drought, habitat conservation, and restoration planning.
sources.title: Rangeland Condition Monitoring, Assessment, and Projection

sources.supports: Describes Earth-observation support for conservation and land-use decisions and ongoing advancement through AI, cloud access, and next-generation satellites.
sources.title: Earth Resources Observation and Science Center

sources.supports: Reports rapid growth in selected federal agencies' AI use cases and identifies policy, privacy, budget, technical-resource, and governance obstacles to adoption.
sources.title: Generative AI Use and Management at Federal Agencies

sources.supports: States that the Great American Outdoors Act authorized $900 million annually in permanent funding for the Land and Water Conservation Fund.
sources.title: Land and Water Conservation Fund

title: Administration of Conservation Programs
